actions.thought: Hand 1 of the tournament. I am on the Button with 5c 7h, which is a weak hand even in heads-up play. While I have position post-flop, the hand strength doesn't justify a raise against an opponent labeled as 'Pro' without more information. Folding is a disciplined choice to start the match, avoiding marginal situations with low-equity hands.

I am on the Button (SB) with [Ad 8c] in a heads-up match, 100bb deep. A8o is a strong starter in this format, ranking well above the median hand. Standard strategy dictates opening with a raise to seize initiative, build the pot, and deny free equity to the Big Blind. A min-raise to 200 is an effective size to accomplish this while keeping the pot manageable.

I am in the Big Blind with T8o. The opponent on the Button (Pro) has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. T8o is a strong enough hand to defend against a wide button opening range at these odds, even though I will be out of position post-flop. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot unnecessarily with a marginal hand.

On a board of Ah-8h-7s, I have middle pair with 8d-Tc. While I have improved, the Ace is a very strong card for the pre-flop aggressor's range. Being out of position, I should check to the button to see how they react. Leading here would likely only get called or raised by better hands (like an Ace) while folding out hands I currently beat. Checking allows me to evaluate their bet sizing and potentially realize my equity or bluff-catch.

I have flopped top two pair (Aces and Eights) on a board with a flush draw ([Ah 8h 7s]). This is a very strong hand in heads-up play. My goal is to extract value from worse Ax hands, pairs like 7x or 8x, and charge the various straight and flush draws. Checking back would give a free card to draws and miss value. A bet of roughly half the pot (200) balances getting value while keeping the opponent's range wide enough to continue with weaker holdings.

I have middle pair (8s) on an Ace-high board after the pre-flop raiser (Pro) made a continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot. This half-pot sizing offers me 4:1 pot odds. While the Ace is a significant part of the Button's range, middle pair is too strong to fold immediately to a single bet. I will call to see a turn card and re-evaluate. Raising is not ideal as it would likely only be called by better hands (Aces) or strong draws (flushes), whereas calling keeps the pot manageable and allows the opponent to continue bluffing with weaker holdings.

The 5c on the turn is a relatively safe card that doesn't significantly change the board texture, although it does slightly connect some straight draws. I still have middle pair with 8d Tc. Since I have been check-calling to control the pot size and the opponent likely has a range advantage with the Ace on the board, checking is the most appropriate action. This allows me to see how the opponent reacts to the turn and potentially reach a showdown or catch bluffs without over-committing to the pot with a marginal hand.

I hold top two pair (Aces and Eights) on a board of [Ah 8h 7s 5c]. The turn card (5c) completes a possible straight for 96 and 64, and gives A5 two pair, but my hand remains very strong against the opponent's wide check-calling range, which includes many single-pair Ax hands, flush draws, and straight draws. Checking back would allow the opponent to realize their equity for free with draws. I need to bet for value and protection. A bet size of 500 (approx. 62% of the pot) extracts value from worse Ax hands and charges draws a poor price to continue.

I have middle pair (8s) with a Ten kicker on an Ace-high board. The opponent, who was the pre-flop aggressor, is firing a second barrel of 500 into an 800 pot. This sizing is significant, but I am getting pot odds of roughly 2.6:1. Since the opponent's button-opening range is wide and includes various semi-bluffs like heart draws or straight draws (e.g., 9x, 6x), folding my middle pair now would be overly tight and exploitable. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate the strength of my hand relative to the final board and my opponent's action.

On the river, the Ten of Spades improves my hand to two pair (Tens and Eights). Throughout the hand, I have been check-calling to manage the pot and catch bluffs. Although I have significantly improved, leading out now (donk betting) would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and only get called by hands that beat me or strong Aces. By checking, I allow the opponent to continue their aggression with missed draws (like the heart flush draw or straight draws) or to value bet an Ace, most of which I now beat. I will check and plan to evaluate their next move, likely looking to check-raise or call depending on their bet sizing.

I hold Top Two Pair (Aces and Eights) on a river board of [Ah 8h 7s 5c Ts]. The river T completes straights for J9 and 96, and gives AT a higher two pair. However, the missed heart flush draw is a significant portion of the opponent's range, as is a weaker Ax (A2-A6, A9, AJ, etc.) which would check-call flop and turn. In a heads-up scenario, Top Two Pair is too strong to check back; I must bet for value targeting those worse Ax hands and lower two pairs (A7, A5). A bet of roughly 55% of the pot (1000 into 1800) is large enough to extract significant value while small enough to encourage calls from top pair hands.

On the river, the Ten of Spades improves my hand to two pair (Tens and Eights). This is a significant improvement, as I now beat all of the opponent's one-pair hands, such as AK, AQ, or AJ, which they likely would have been value-betting on all three streets. However, I must be cautious because the opponent's range also includes several two-pair combinations that beat mine (AT, A8, A7, A5) as well as sets (77, 55) or a possible straight (96). By betting 1000 into 1800, Pro is showing significant strength. If I raise, I likely only get called by hands that have me beaten, whereas calling allows me to win against their bluffs (missed heart draws) and their one-pair value bets. Given the board texture and the triple-barrel line from the opponent, a call is the most prudent action to maximize value while controlling risk.

Ace-Six offsuit is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, especially from the button. After losing a significant pot in the previous hand by calling down, I need to assert initiative. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that allows me to take control of the betting lead and potentially win the blinds or play a pot with a range advantage.

I hold A8o in the Big Blind against a 3bb button raise. This is a top 20-30% hand in heads-up play and well ahead of a typical wide opening range. Pot odds require 25% equity (calling 200 to win 600), and A8o has significantly more than that. While a 3-bet is possible, calling is the standard play to keep the opponent's range wide and avoid bloating the pot out of position with a marginal showdown hand.

I am out of position on a paired board (7c 7d 4d) with Ace-high (Ac 8s). In heads-up play, Ace-high has significant showdown value on this texture, as it is difficult for the opponent to connect with the board. However, leading out (donk betting) is generally suboptimal as it folds out the opponent's air (which I beat) and isolates me against better hands. The standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to induce bluffs or allow them to check back. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate the sizing, but my hand is currently too strong to fold to a standard c-bet.

Ace-high is currently likely the best hand, and as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continuation bet on this paired board to maintain initiative. A small bet of 200 (1/3 pot) allows me to fold out his weakest hands while keeping the pot manageable if he has a piece of the board or a draw.

I hold A8o (Ace-high) on a paired board of 7-7-4. In heads-up play, Ace-high is a strong bluff-catcher against a wide button opening range. The opponent has made a small continuation bet of 200 into 600 (1/3 pot). My hand is well ahead of his pure bluffs (like KQ, JT, or lower air) and has decent equity against some draws. Folding is incorrect given the pot odds (20% equity required) and my relative hand strength. Raising would likely only get called by better hands or strong draws, isolating me against a stronger range. Calling allows me to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn brings a second 4, making the board 7c 7d 4d 4h. This double-paired board significantly increases the value of my Ace-high. My hand is now Two Pair (Sevens and Fours) with an Ace kicker (7-7-4-4-A). This combination beats any lower pocket pairs like 22 or 33 (who would play 7-7-4-4-low kicker) and all non-paired hands (King-high, etc.). I only lose to pocket pairs 55+ or a player holding a 4 or 7. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out the weaker hands I am crushing and get action only from hands that beat me. Checking is the most +EV play to keep the opponent's bluffing range wide and potentially induce a bet from inferior hands trying to represent strength.

The board is now double-paired (7-7-4-4). My Ace-high still has significant showdown value. Checking back on the turn allows me to control the pot size and reach a showdown cheaply, while avoiding a potential check-raise on a board where my hand is unlikely to improve further.

On the river, the board reads 7c 7d 4d 4h Qs. This is a double-paired board (Sevens and Fours) with a Queen kicker. My hand is Ace-high (Ac 8s), which makes my best five-card hand 7-7-4-4-A. This beats any King-high or lower, as well as pocket pairs 22 and 33 (which are counterfeited by the board and play 7-7-4-4-Q, losing to my Ace kicker). However, the Queen is a significant card for the opponent's range; any Qx hand now makes Queens up (Q-Q-7-7-4) and beats me. Additionally, pocket pairs 55+ beat me. Since the opponent checked back the turn, their range is somewhat capped, but the river card favors their wide button range which contains many Queens. Betting here serves no purpose: worse hands (K-high) will fold, and better hands (Qx, pairs) will call. Checking is the optimal line to try to get to showdown cheaply or evaluate if the opponent bets representing the Queen.

On this double-paired board (7-7-4-4-Q), my Ace-high has decent showdown value. Both of us checked the turn, suggesting neither has a strong hand like a 7 or a 4. Pro's check on the river further indicates weakness or a desire to reach showdown. Betting wouldn't likely get called by worse hands and might invite a bluff-raise. Checking back is the most prudent way to win the pot if my Ace-high is ahead.

In heads-up play, K6o is a hand well within the standard button opening range (typically top 70-80% of hands). I have the positional advantage and a King blocker. My opponent defended their big blind in Hand 2 but folded in Hand 1, suggesting they are not defending 100% of hands. A minimum raise to 200 exerts pressure while risking little, allowing me to take down the pot pre-flop or play in position with the initiative. Folding is too tight here, and limping is passive given my chip advantage.

I am holding Ace-Six offsuit in the Big Blind. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200, requiring a call of 100 more. This is a standard defense situation given the favorable pot odds (3:1). A6o is well within the defending range against a Button open. I will call to see the flop and evaluate based on board texture.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace and a 6 kicker on a board of Qs-2s-Ad. This is a strong hand, but vulnerable to better Aces. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard and most strategic play here to see how they react to the Ace-high board. It allows me to control the pot size and catch potential bluffs or continuation bets from the opponent.

I hold the King of Spades on an Ace-Queen high board with two spades (Qs 2s Ad), giving me the second-nut flush draw. While this board texture favors my range as the pre-flop aggressor and a c-bet would be standard, checking back is a superior play here for several reasons. First, it allows me to realize my equity for free, ensuring I see the turn card. Second, it controls the pot size; I do not want to face a check-raise which would force me to make a tough decision with just a draw. Third, checking back flush draws balances my checking range, preventing the opponent from automatically attacking when I check. Given the opponent's 'sticky' play in Hand 2, avoiding a semi-bluff into a potential calling station is prudent.

I have improved to trips with the second Ace appearing on the turn. Since the opponent checked back on the flop, it's unlikely they hold a very strong Ace, and they are more likely to have a Queen, a pocket pair, or a draw. I will lead out with a bet of 200 (half pot) to extract value from these hands and protect against the flush draw.

The turn card [Ac] pairs the board, nullifying any backdoor flush potential and leaving me with the board's pair of Aces and a King kicker. The opponent's lead for half-pot into a checked-back board is a strong indicator of value, likely representing Trip Aces, a Queen, or a mid-to-low pocket pair, all of which dominate my King-high. Although the pot odds (3:1) are reasonable, my hand has virtually no playability against a 'sticky' opponent who is now showing aggression. Floating here would likely lead to a difficult decision on the river. Folding is the disciplined option to preserve my chip stack.

I am on the button with Ace-Five offsuit, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot. I previously raised to 300 with A6c, so I will maintain that sizing for consistency.

I hold Q4o in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 total) from the Button. While heads-up requires defending a wide range, Q4o is in the bottom 25-30% of hands and performs poorly out of position, especially against a larger-than-minimum raise. The pot odds require 33% equity, but equity realization will be difficult OOP with a disjointed, weak hand. Folding is the standard and disciplined play here to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the Button with Qh7h (Queen-Seven suited), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, well above the median hand strength. In this position, standard strategy dictates playing aggressively to steal the blinds or build a pot with position. My opponent has shown some passivity in calling down previous hands. Raising allows me to take the initiative. I will open with a standard min-raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Nine of diamonds, which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. The opponent raised to 2BB from the button. Calling is the most standard play here to see a flop with a hand that has good post-flop playability and potential to hit flushes or straights, while also keeping the pot size controlled against an aggressive opponent.

I have a gutshot straight draw (needs a Ten) and a backdoor diamond flush draw on a board of Kc-Jd-6c. As the Big Blind who called a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor to see how they react to this board, which likely hits their range well. Checking allows me to control the pot and decide my next move based on Pro's action.

I hold Queen-high (Qh7h) on a K-J-6 board. While I have the range advantage as the pre-flop raiser, this board texture (two broadway cards) hits the Big Blind's calling range reasonably well. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten) and a backdoor flush draw. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hitting a Ten or Queen on the turn, while avoiding getting check-raised. It also controls the pot size with a marginal hand that has some showdown value against missed draws.

The turn card is the 4 of clubs, putting a third club on the board. I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 10 for a King-high straight). Since the flop was checked through by both players, it is unlikely the opponent has a very strong hand, but they could easily have a King, a Jack, or a draw. I will check here to see if I can get a free river card to complete my straight or potentially win with a Queen if the board remains favorable. Checking also allows me to control the size of the pot with a hand that currently has very little showdown value.

On the Turn, the board is Jd 6c Kc 4c. There are three clubs on the board, making a flush possible. I hold Qh7h, giving me Queen-high with no flush draw (I have hearts) and no meaningful straight draw (K-J-Q needs T-9 or T-A). My opponent checked the flop and has now checked the turn. While their checking indicates weakness or fear of the flush, my hand has very little equity if called. Bluffing here represents the flush, but passive opponents often call with pairs (Kx, Jx, or even 6x/4x) or a high club. Checking back allows me to try to get to showdown cheaply with Queen-high, potentially winning against missed straight draws or lower high-cards, or hitting a Queen on the river. There is no need to bloat the pot with a marginal hand.

I have Queen-high on a board of Jd-6c-Kc-4c-Kh. Both the flop and turn were checked through by both players, which suggests that neither of us has a particularly strong hand. While my hand has no showdown value (losing to any pair or Ace-high), a bluff on this board is risky because my opponent could easily have a weak Jack or a small pair that they were planning to check down. Given the small pot size, I will check and hope for a check-back, though I am prepared to fold if the opponent bets, as I have no piece of this board.

I hold Queen-high (Qh7h) on a board of Jd 6c Kc 4c Kh. The board pairs the King on the river and contains three clubs. My opponent (Flash) has checked to me on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River). While this indicates weakness, my own line of checking back the Flop and Turn also indicates a lack of strength (likely capping my range). My Queen-high has a small amount of showdown value against missed straight draws or lower high cards that the Big Blind might defend with. However, trying to bluff here is risky because my passive line makes it difficult to credibly represent a strong King or a Flush, and previous hands suggest the opponent is capable of calling down with marginal holdings (like second pair in Hand #2). Betting would likely only fold out hands I already beat or split with (Ace-high), while getting called by any pair (6x, 4x, Jx). Therefore, checking back to realize whatever equity I have and minimizing loss is the best play.

89o is a solid hand to open from the button in heads-up play. I want to apply pressure and take control of the hand. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop.

The opponent has raised to 3BB (300), which offers worse pot odds than a standard min-raise. I am out of position with 7s6d (offsuit connectors). While this hand has some straight potential, defending 76o out of position against a 3x raise is generally -EV due to the difficulty of realizing equity. Needing 33% raw equity to call is technically met against a wide range, but realizing that equity OOP is the main hurdle. I will fold to preserve chips for better spots.

In a Heads-Up match, 97o falls within the standard opening range (top ~65-70% of hands). While speculative, folding the button too frequently is exploitable. The opponent (Flash) has shown a willingness to defend their Big Blind (Hands #2 and #4) but has also folded the Button (Hand #1), indicating they are not playing 100% of hands. Raising to 200 (min-raise) is a low-risk play that risks 150 to win 150. It maintains my aggressive table image, gives me the initiative, and ensures I play in position post-flop if called. Given the stack depth (113BB) and chip lead, I can comfortably apply pressure while easily folding to a 3-bet.

Holding 9h 3c in the Big Blind, I am facing a 2x open from the Button. While the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a 300 pot), 93o is a very weak hand that realizes its equity poorly out of position. Given the early stage of the match and the disadvantage of playing a low-equity hand against a skilled opponent, I will make the disciplined fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 85o. While I have position post-flop, this hand is quite weak and doesn't play well against a standard Big Blind defending range. Looking at my previous play, I have folded similar hands like 57o and 93o. I will fold here to maintain a solid image and avoid playing a marginal hand out of position pre-flop.

In a heads-up match, the button range should be wide (opening 70-80% or more). J7o is a marginal hand (approx top 47%) but has sufficient equity to open-raise, especially given the position and the opponent's previous fold in the Big Blind to a raise with a weaker hand (Hand #8 with 97o). I have raised every button so far and the opponent has not yet 3-bet, suggesting they are passive. Raising allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop or use position to outplay post-flop. A min-raise to 200 is the standard sizing.

I am in the Big Blind with Q3o. The opponent on the Button has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, Q3o is a very weak hand that realizes its equity poorly out of position. I have been playing relatively tight-aggressive and don't want to start defending with marginal hands that can easily be dominated or lead to difficult post-flop decisions. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better spots.

I have Ten of Spades and Six of Spades on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a strong enough hand to open for a raise, especially given that Pro has folded to several of my pre-flop raises in previous hands. Raising to 300 maintains consistency with my earlier button play.

I am in the Big Blind holding 8c 6h facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring ~25% equity to call, but playing this weak off-suit one-gapper out of position against a larger-than-minimum raise size is generally unprofitable. I previously folded 7s 6d to a similar raise, and 8c 6h is structurally weaker. Folding is the prudent play.

I hold King-Four suited (K4s) on the button. In heads-up play, this is a very playable hand due to the King blocker and flush potential. My opponent has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises (Hands 1, 8, 10), so a standard min-raise creates fold equity. If called, I have position and a hand that can flop well. Raising to 200 is the standard play here.

I have Ace-Deuce offsuit in the Big Blind. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot is offering 3-to-1 odds, and Ace-high hands have decent equity against a wide button opening range. I will call to defend my blind and see the flop.

I have Ace-high on a low, connected board (3c-6d-7s). I am currently out of position. The opponent (Pro) has not yet acted on the flop. Since I am first to act, checking is the standard play with my entire range here to see how the opponent reacts, as this board can hit a flat-calling range but also allows the pre-flop aggressor to continuation bet. I have one overcard (the Ace) and a backdoor wheel draw, but my hand is mostly a bluff-catcher or a hand with limited showdown value. I will check to the raiser.

I hold Kd4d on a flop of 3c 6d 7s. The board texture is low and connected, which favors the Big Blind's calling range (connecting with 45, 56, 67, sets, and pairs). While I have King-high, a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5), and a backdoor flush draw, this is a board where c-betting frequently faces resistance or check-raises. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a Diamond, King, or 5 on the turn, and controls the pot size with a marginal hand. I avoid being blown off my equity by a check-raise.

On the turn 10 of diamonds, the board remains relatively low and disconnected, though the 10 provides a new pair possibility. After the flop went check-check, it's likely that neither of us has a strong hand. My Ace-high still maintains reasonable showdown value against many of the opponent's misses (like King-high or Queen-high). I will check again to see if the opponent wants to lead or if we can get to a cheap showdown. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to be called by worse hands, and I don't want to be forced to fold my equity if raised.

On the turn, the board is [3c 6d 7s Td]. I have improved significantly, picking up a King-high flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) to go with my King-high. While I have plenty of outs (approx. 15), my hand currently is just King-high. The Ten on the turn connects somewhat with the Big Blind's defensive range (Tx hands). Betting here risks a check-raise, which would be disastrous as I would have to fold my substantial equity or commit heavily with a drawing hand. Checking back guarantees I see the river card for free, fully realizing my equity. If I hit my flush or straight on the river, I can bet for value or call a bet. If I miss, I have kept the pot small and can easily fold or check it down.

The board has paired on the river (7c), making the final board 3-6-7-T-7. After the action went check-check on both the flop and the turn, it is highly likely that neither of us has a strong hand. My Ace-high (As2c) holds significant showdown value in this specific line. Betting is unlikely to be called by worse hands, and checking allows me to realize my equity for free or potentially catch a bluff if the opponent decides to bet their missed overcards. I will check.

I arrived at the river in a checked-down pot with King-high (King-Four). The board is 3c 6d 7s Td 7c. The 7 on the river pairs the board. My flush and straight draws have missed. My hand has showdown value against the opponent's missed straight draws (like 45, 58, 89) and random lower high-cards (Q-high, J-high). Betting here would be a bluff, aiming to fold out Ace-high or perhaps a weak pair like 3x or 6x, but given the opponent's previous tendency to call down (Hand #2), there is a risk he calls with any pair. Since I beat his complete air and checking costs nothing, realizing my showdown equity is the prudent play in this small pot.

I am on the button with K3o. While it's a weak King, in heads-up play, any King has significant value. Looking at the history, the opponent (Pro) has folded to several of my 3x raises from the small blind (Hands #5, #7, #11). I will maintain this aggressive posture and raise to 300 to try and take the pot down pre-flop or play the hand in position.

I am in the Big Blind with KTo, facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. KTo is a strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top 20-30% of hands, and has significantly more than the 33% equity required to call the additional 200 chips into a pot of 400. While a 3-bet is an option, calling is the standard play to keep the pot manageable while out of position and to keep the opponent's range wide. I will call to see a flop.

I am out of position on the flop with King-high and a backdoor flush draw (Kd). The board (9h 8d 2d) is fairly coordinated. Standard heads-up strategy involves checking to the pre-flop aggressor to see their action. I have enough equity to potentially continue if the bet is small or check-raise later, but leading out here is unnecessary and exposes me to being raised. I will check.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and have position. The board of 9h-8d-2d is somewhat coordinated but doesn't clearly favor the Big Blind's calling range more than my opening range. By betting 300 (half the pot), I can often take the pot down immediately if the opponent missed the flop. If called, I still have two overcards to the board that could provide value on the turn.

I am facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) on a 9h 8d 2d board. I hold Kd Th (King-Ten offsuit). I have two overcards to the board and a backdoor flush draw with the King of diamonds. I need 25% equity to call (300 to win a total pot of 1200). Against a standard wide button opening and c-betting range, my overcards and backdoor equity typically provide significantly more than 25% equity. Folding here would be too weak given my hand strength relative to my checking range. A raise is too risky as I have little fold equity against made hands or strong draws and do not want to build a large pot with just King-high. Therefore, calling is the most profitable play to realize my equity on the turn.

The turn card is the 9c, pairing the board (9h 8d 2d 9c). I hold King-high (KdTh). While the board pairing reduces the likelihood of the opponent holding a 9 (due to blockers), I am still out of position with a marginal made hand that only beats missed draws. Leading out (donk betting) would likely only fold out hands I am already beating and isolate me against better hands. Checking is the standard line to control the pot size and re-evaluate based on the opponent's action.

The turn 9c pairs the top card of the board. Having been the pre-flop and flop aggressor, I have the range advantage, but Pro's check-call on the flop suggests they have a piece of this board, likely an 8, a small pocket pair, or a diamond draw. While the 9 is a good card to represent, my King-high holds some showdown value against the various draws in Pro's range (like diamond draws or straight draws). By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for a potential King or 3 on the river, and avoid being check-raised off my hand by a 9 or a slow-played monster.

I have improved to top two pair (Tens and Nines) on the river with a King kicker. The board is now [9h 8d 2d 9c Ts]. The opponent's decision to check back the turn strongly suggests they do not hold a Nine or a strong made hand, likely capping their range to draws, marginal pairs, or air. However, the river Ten completes straight draws for JQ and 76, and gives Ace-Ten a better two pair. Betting for value is thin because better hands (Straights, AT, 9x) will call or raise, while worse hands (8x, missed diamonds) will likely fold. Checking is the superior option to induce bluffs from missed flush draws and to avoid isolating myself against the top of the opponent's range. I am prepared to call a reasonable bet given my hand strength relative to the opponent's perceived range.

The river is a Ten of Spades, making the board 9-8-2-9-10. Pro has checked to me twice after calling my flop bet. My King-high has some showdown value against missed draws (like diamond draws or QJ/J7 which didn't get there, although J-10 did). Given that Pro check-called the flop, they likely have a piece of the board (an 8, a 2, or a 9) or a draw. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a pair, and Pro might be trapping with a 9 or an 8. I will check behind to realize my showdown value and avoid losing more chips if I am beaten.

In heads-up play, K4o is a standard opening hand from the Button due to positional advantage and the wide range of hands required to be competitive. I have been raising my buttons consistently (to 200), and my opponent has folded to some of these raises or played passively post-flop. I want to apply pressure and utilize my position. A min-raise to 200 is the most effective way to either take down the pot pre-flop or play a raised pot in position.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 5s, a suited gapper that plays reasonably well in heads-up situations. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button.
